worth_it_explanation: Imported conversions from Google Analytics 4 (GA4) feed Smart Bidding directly, so a duplicate count, a mismatched attribution window, or a weak key event imported as a primary conversion all distort what the algorithm learns and bids toward. The checks here are configuration reviews, not rebuilds. Spotting a duplicate or a wrong key event import requires a few minutes in the Google Ads UI and the GA4 link settings, and the correction is a configuration change that takes effect on new conversions.
intro: Imported conversions bring Google Analytics 4 (GA4) key events into Google Ads as conversion actions, letting the two platforms share a single measurement source. These checks confirm that the import is set up to count the right things once, attribute them consistently, and send Smart Bidding a signal worth optimizing toward.

audit_checks.title: A GA4-imported conversion and a Google Ads tag both count the same action
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the same purchase or lead fires both a GA4-imported conversion and a native Google Ads tag conversion, the action counts twice in the 'All conversions' column. Smart Bidding learns from an inflated signal, which can push bids and budgets higher than the real performance supports.

educate.base:
Google Ads lets advertisers import key events from Google Analytics 4 (GA4) as conversion actions. At the same time, the account may also run a Google Ads conversion tag on the same page, placed there before the GA4 import was configured. When both are active and both are marked as primary conversion actions, every qualifying visit records the same action twice.

The duplication shows in the 'All conversions' column rather than the 'Conversions' column if the Ads tag is set to secondary. But if both are set to primary, Smart Bidding sees a count that is roughly double the real one and calibrates bids accordingly. The effect is most visible on purchase confirmations and lead form thank-you pages, where both the tag and the GA4 event fire in close proximity.

This happens when the GA4 import is added later, while the original Ads tag is left in place rather than paused or removed. Both then count independently, with no deduplication between them.

Signs that duplication may be present:
  • A 'Conversions' column value that exceeds what the GA4 'Key events' report shows for the same date range
  • A conversion action list that includes both an imported GA4 key event and a Google Ads tag for the same action name or event type
  • The conversion action summary shows two primary actions of similar volume for the same conversion category
You may want to confirm which source is the intended one before pausing anything. If the GA4 import is the preferred source, consider pausing or removing the Ads tag conversion action and verifying that the import continues to record at the expected volume.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, purchase is the conversion most likely to carry this duplication. The purchase confirmation page often has both a legacy Google Ads purchase tag and a GA4 'purchase' key event that was later imported. You may want to compare the Google Ads 'Conversions' column to GA4's 'Key events' count for the same action over the same period. A ratio far above 1:1 is a sign that both sources are counting.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the form submission thank-you page is the point to check. The same form submission may fire both a GA4 'generate_lead' key event (imported into Google Ads) and an Ads tag placed on the confirmation URL. You may want to open Tag Assistant on the confirmation page and list the conversion events it records to see whether two separate conversion actions fire for one form fill.

learn_more.source_title: About conversion actions in Google Ads

investigate.intro: List all active conversion actions and identify any that track the same underlying user action through both a GA4 import and a Google Ads tag.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Goals', then 'Conversions', and view the full list of conversion actions.

Look for pairs where one action is sourced from 'Google Analytics 4' and another is sourced from 'Website' (meaning a Google Ads tag). Note whether any pairs share a conversion category, such as 'Purchase' or 'Submit lead form'.
For each suspected pair, check the 'Primary / secondary' column. If both are set to primary, they both contribute to the 'Conversions' column and feed Smart Bidding.
Compare the 'Conversions' column total for the date range to what GA4 shows under Reports > Engagement > Events for the equivalent key event. A ratio well above 1:1 supports the duplication.

google_tag_assistant.label: Google Tag Assistant
steps: Open Tag Assistant and connect it to a page that fires the conversion, for example a purchase confirmation or a form thank-you page.
In the events panel, count how many distinct Google Ads conversion events fire for a single action. Two separate conversion events for one page visit confirm the duplication.

google_analytics_4.label: Google Analytics 4
steps: Open GA4 and go to Reports > Engagement > Key events.
Find the key event that corresponds to the GA4-imported conversion and note its total for the date range.
Compare that count to the same conversion action's volume in Google Ads for the same period. If Google Ads shows a count meaningfully higher than GA4, a second source is also counting.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner lists each conversion action and its source (GA4 import or Google Ads tag). For any action where both sources count the same event and both are set to primary, the check fails. The fail is recorded with the conversion action names and their sources. If one is already set to secondary, note it but mark as a lower-severity finding since it affects 'All conversions' but not 'Conversions'.
evaluate.fallback_message: List the conversion actions, their sources, and their primary or secondary status above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is counting at least one conversion action twice: once through a GA4-imported key event and once through a Google Ads conversion tag tracking the same user action. When both sources are set as primary, Smart Bidding receives a signal that overstates the real conversion volume, which can push bids and budgets above what the actual performance supports. {{voice_recommend}} deciding which source should be the single source of record for each conversion action, then pausing or removing the duplicate, and confirming that the retained source records at the expected volume after the change.

audit_checks.title: GA4-imported conversions use GA4 attribution and lookback, which differ from the Google Ads native defaults
audit_checks.impact_rationale: GA4 and Google Ads attribute the same conversion to different touchpoints when their attribution models or lookback windows differ. Smart Bidding then learns from a credit distribution that does not match what the native tracking would have produced, which shifts bids in ways that can reduce efficiency without a clear signal of why.

educate.base:
When a Google Analytics 4 (GA4) key event is imported into Google Ads as a conversion action, Google Ads uses the attribution model and lookback window set in GA4, not the model or window the Google Ads conversion action would have used on its own.

GA4's default attribution model is data-driven, with a click-through lookback of 30 days and a view-through lookback of 1 day. Google Ads native conversion actions can be configured with different defaults, including a 30-day or 60-day click lookback and a separate view-through window. If the two accounts are configured with different windows or models, the same conversion can attribute credit to different campaigns and ad groups depending on which source recorded it.

The practical effect is that performance reports can show different conversion counts for the same period when comparing the GA4 report to the Google Ads campaign report. Analysts and media buyers looking at both tend to reconcile the numbers manually, without always recognizing that the difference comes from the attribution configuration rather than from a measurement error.

Smart Bidding sees the credit distribution that the GA4 attribution model produces, so it optimizes toward the touchpoints GA4 weighted, not necessarily toward the touchpoints a Google Ads native model would have weighted. Whether that is better or worse depends on which model is more accurate for the account, but the difference should be a deliberate choice rather than a side effect of an import.

You may want to confirm that the GA4 attribution settings match what the account intends for Google Ads bidding, and document the difference so that reporting comparisons between the two platforms are interpreted correctly.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store with longer purchase cycles, the lookback window choice has a larger effect. A purchase researched over several sessions may credit a different touchpoint depending on whether the lookback is 30 or 60 days. You may want to confirm that the GA4 click lookback window covers the typical purchase cycle length for the product category.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site where brand campaigns assist later direct conversions, the attribution model affects how much credit search brand campaigns receive. A data-driven model may weight the last paid click more than a linear model would. You may want to compare performance reports under both models in GA4 before committing the imported conversion to Smart Bidding.

learn_more.source_title: About attribution models for imported Google Analytics conversions

investigate.intro: Compare the attribution model and lookback window in GA4 to the settings on the corresponding Google Ads conversion action, and note any differences that affect how Smart Bidding distributes credit.

google_analytics_4.label: Google Analytics 4
steps: In GA4, open Admin, then go to 'Attribution settings' under the Property column.
Note the reporting attribution model (e.g., 'Data-driven', 'Last click') and the lookback windows for engaged-click and session conversions.
These settings apply to all key events imported into Google Ads from this GA4 property.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: In Google Ads, open 'Goals', then 'Conversions', and select a conversion action that is sourced from GA4.

In the conversion action's settings, find the attribution model and lookback window fields. Note that for imported GA4 conversions, these reflect the GA4 property settings rather than a standalone Google Ads configuration.
Compare what you see to the GA4 attribution settings you noted above and record any difference in model, click lookback window, or view-through window.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the GA4 attribution model and lookback windows and compares them to what the account intends for Google Ads bidding. If the settings match the account's documented intent, the check passes. If the settings differ from a native Google Ads conversion action that was previously used, or if no intentional choice was made about the GA4 attribution settings before the import was created, the check flags the mismatch for review. The evaluation note includes the model name and both lookback windows.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the GA4 attribution model and lookback windows alongside the intended Google Ads settings above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} imports GA4 key events into Google Ads as conversion actions, and the attribution model and lookback windows those imports carry from GA4 differ from the Google Ads native conversion settings the account used previously. Smart Bidding now learns from the credit distribution GA4 produces, which weights touchpoints differently than the prior configuration. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the GA4 attribution settings in Admin and confirming that the model and lookback windows are an intentional choice for how the account wants to optimize, then documenting the settings so that future reporting comparisons between GA4 and Google Ads are interpreted with the attribution difference in mind.

audit_checks.title: GA4 is not linked to Google Ads, or the imported key event is a weak signal for bidding
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An account that imports no GA4 conversions misses the attribution benefit of GA4's cross-device and cross-session tracking. An account that imports a soft event, such as a pageview or a 'scroll' event, directs Smart Bidding toward a signal that does not reflect business value, which can inflate conversion volume while actual revenue or lead quality stays flat.

educate.base:
Importing key events from Google Analytics 4 (GA4) into Google Ads requires two things: a product link between the GA4 property and the Google Ads account, and at least one key event in GA4 that has been selected for import. If the link is absent, the import option is not available. If the link exists but no key event has been imported, or if the imported event is a soft engagement metric rather than a meaningful business outcome, the account is either missing the import benefit or directing Smart Bidding toward the wrong thing.

GA4 makes it straightforward to mark any event as a key event, including events such as 'page_view', 'scroll', or 'session_start' that fire for almost every visit. If one of these is then imported into Google Ads as a primary conversion, Smart Bidding treats every such event as a goal and bids up for traffic that triggers it, whether or not that traffic converts to anything of business value.

The link between GA4 and Google Ads is established in GA4 under Admin > Product Links > Google Ads Links, or in Google Ads under Tools > Linked accounts. Both paths lead to the same connection. If the connection exists, the available key events appear in Google Ads under Goals > Conversions > New conversion action > Import from Google Analytics.

Signs that this check applies:
  • The Google Ads account has no conversion actions sourced from GA4, and no product link appears in either account
  • The account imports a key event with a very high volume relative to known purchase or lead volume, suggesting a soft engagement event
  • The key event name in the conversion action list is a GA4 automatically collected event name rather than a custom event tied to a business outcome
You may want to review which key event is imported and confirm that it corresponds to an action with measurable business value before leaving it as a primary conversion.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the key event most worth importing is the GA4 'purchase' event, which GA4 records when the ecommerce purchase parameters are populated. If the account instead imports a 'view_item' or 'add_to_cart' event as a primary conversion, Smart Bidding receives a signal from users who browsed but did not buy. You may want to confirm the imported event name matches the transaction-level event rather than a funnel step.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the key event worth importing is one tied to a completed form submission or a qualified lead, not a page visit or a video play. You may want to confirm the imported event fires only when the form reaches a true confirmation state, not on every visit to the form page.

learn_more.source_title: Link Google Analytics to Google Ads
Mark events as key events in GA4

investigate.intro: Confirm that GA4 is linked to Google Ads and that the imported key event represents a meaningful business outcome rather than a soft engagement metric.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Tools', then 'Linked accounts', and look for Google Analytics 4 in the list. If no GA4 property appears, the accounts are not linked and no import is possible.
Open 'Goals', then 'Conversions', and filter the conversion action list by source. Look for any actions listed as imported from 'Google Analytics 4'.

For each imported conversion action, note the event name and compare it to the event names GA4 automatically collects, such as 'page_view', 'scroll', or 'session_start'. An automatic event imported as a primary conversion is a signal quality problem.

Check the conversion volume of the imported action against the account's reported revenue or lead volume. A conversion count far higher than known outcomes suggests a soft event is being counted as a primary goal.

google_analytics_4.label: Google Analytics 4
steps: In GA4, open Admin, then go to 'Product Links' and select 'Google Ads Links'. Confirm the correct Google Ads account appears and the link status is active.
Go to Admin, then 'Events', and view the key events list. Confirm that any event marked as a key event and intended for Google Ads import corresponds to a meaningful business outcome.
Note the volume of the key event under Reports > Engagement > Key events and compare it to what the Google Ads conversion action shows for the same period to confirm the import is active.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner first confirms whether a GA4 product link exists. If no link exists, the check fails with a note that no import is possible. If a link exists, the practitioner reviews the imported key event name and confirms it corresponds to a business outcome rather than a GA4 automatically collected event. An event name matching GA4's automatically collected event list imported as a primary conversion is a fail. An event name tied to a purchase, a form submission, or a confirmed lead and imported as primary passes.
evaluate.fallback_message: Confirm the GA4 product link status and the imported key event name above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} an issue with the GA4-to-Google Ads conversion import for {{site_name}}. Either the GA4 property is not linked to the Google Ads account, leaving imported conversions unavailable, or the key event imported as a primary conversion is a soft engagement metric rather than a meaningful business outcome. When Smart Bidding optimizes toward a signal that does not reflect actual revenue or qualified leads, it can increase traffic and conversion volume without improving the underlying business results. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the GA4 product link is active, reviewing which key event is marked as a key event in GA4 and imported into Google Ads, and replacing any soft engagement event with the event that best represents the business outcome the account should optimize toward.
